data.title: Supreme Court decision sets up battle over tariff refunds: What to know
data.description:
The Supreme Court’s blockbuster decision invalidating President Trump’s tariffs is expected to kick off laborious efforts by importers to get refunds for the billions of dollars they’ve paid over the past year. 

The court’s decision, however, gives no guidance. 

As Justice Amy Coney Barrett put it at oral arguments, it’s set to be “a mess.” 

Here’s what to know as the process gets underway: 

Supreme Court didn’t address refunds 

The court’s opinion doesn’t address refunds, leaving importers to fight with the federal government and lower courts to figure it out.   

It was only Justice Brett Kavanaugh, writing in dissent, who noted the incoming battle. 

“One issue will be refunds,” Kavanaugh wrote. “Refunds of billions of dollars would have significant consequences for the U.S. Treasury. The Court says nothing today about whether, and if so how, the Government should go about returning the billions of dollars that it has collected from importers.” 

More than $175 billion is at risk of being refunded, Penn-Wharton Budget Model economists said in a recent analysis first reported by Reuters.

Refunds won’t go directly to consumers 

However it shakes out, any refunds are set to be paid back to importers. Not consumers. 

It’s already led some Democrats to begin calling for consumers to share in any of the returned funds. 

“Any refunds must end up in the pockets of the Americans who were illegally cheated out of their money by Donald Trump,” Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) wrote on social platform X. 

Ahead of the Supreme Court decision, Trump himself had floated sending collected tariff revenue to Americans as rebate payments. 

But that collected revenue may now be back on its way to companies. 

Trump administration has insisted it will provide refunds  

As litigation piled up in the courts, the Trump administration repeatedly insisted it would provide full refunds — with interest — to importers who pays a tariff later invalidated by the Supreme Court. 

The administration has dismissed anyone who has insinuated their refund is at risk. 

Last May, the Justice Department entered a binding legal agreement guaranteeing refunds to a group of businesses led by Princess Awesome, a Maryland-based company that seeks to break gender stereotypes in kids apparel. 

In June, the administration told another group of small businesses they “will not lose their entitlement to a refund.”  

And they’ve continued to reaffirm the position in the months since in various other lawsuits. 

The U.S. Court of International Trade, which will oversee the refund process, has already made clear the Trump administration’s position is set in stone. 

“Having convinced this court to accept that importers who paid IEEPA tariffs will be able to receive refunds after reliquidation … the Government cannot later ‘assume a contrary position,’” a panel on the court wrote in a December order. 

Companies are already lined up 

As the justices mulled their decision, companies began lining up by the dozen in court. 

The list includes entities affiliated with Costco, Toyota, Goodyear Tires, Columbia Sportswear, Barnes & Noble, Logitech, Marathon Petroleum, Revlon, Teladoc Health, PopSockets, Peloton, Dole, J. Crew, GoPro, Bath & Body Works, Steve Madden, Crocs, Xerox and YETI. 

Hundreds have sued in total, including a host of small businesses who’ve forked over payments since Trump began his global trade war last year. 

They didn’t want to wait for the Supreme Court’s decision over concerns they’d be losing their right to claim a refund. Some previous rulings suggest companies may need to sue before customs authorities finalize their tariff, a step called “liquidation.” 

Given the government’s refund assurances, however, the trade court put all the lawsuits on hold until the Supreme Court’s decision. 

They’re set to resume with the high court’s decision now in hand. 

DOJ hopes to streamline lawsuits, but questions remain 

With an ever-expanding pileup of lawsuits, the Justice Department has suggested streamlining things. 

It has floated following how the courts handled thousands of challenges to the tariffs on China Trump imposed during his first term. 

In that scenario, the trade court would recognize a steering committee of attorneys to represent all the businesses and sit across the table from the government in any negotiations. 

The makeup of that committee has yet to be decided. The government indicated it’s ok if the businesses are the ones to propose the list. 

But some contenders are already apparent. A team at law firm Crowell & Moring is behind dozens of the lawsuits, including the one that has emerged as the lead case. Rock Trade law and several small firms are backing many of the others. 

Other contours of the process remain to be seen, including what will be managed by the court versus customs officials, and the specific steps companies who haven’t sued must take to secure their funds. 

The Justice Department told the court it should set up a tab on its website to make updates easily accessible.

data.title: Trump calls Supreme Court justices who ruled against tariffs 'disloyal'
data.description:
President Trump slammed the Supreme Court justices that struck down his tariffs on Friday, which included two justices he appointed, saying he was “ashamed of certain members of the court.” 

“They’re very unpatriotic and disloyal to our Constitution. It’s my opinion that the court has been swayed by foreign interests and a political movement that is far smaller than people would ever think. It’s a small movement,” Trump told reporters at a White House press briefing he held in the aftermath of the momentous opinion.

“I’d like to thank and congratulate Justices Thomas Alito and [Brett] Kavanaugh for their strength and wisdom and love of our country, which is right now, very proud of those justices,” he said, referring to the dissenting justices. Trump also appointed Kavanaugh during his first term as president.

In a 6-3 ruling, the justices rejected Trump’s expanded use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) in imposing tariffs on nearly every country. The 1970s law allows the president to “regulate” imports when necessary to respond to national emergencies that pose an “unusual and extraordinary” threat.  

DEVELOPING

data.title: VA reverses on new disability rating rule after intense backlash
data.description:
The Department of Veterans Affairs this week abruptly reversed course on a new rule requiring veterans’ disability ratings to be calculated by how well they function on medication and not on the underlying condition or injury itself, a stance that was quickly condemned. 

“Effective immediately, VA is halting enforcement of the interim final rule, ‘Evaluative Rating: Impact of Medication,’” VA Secretary Doug Collins said in a statement posted to social media Thursday afternoon.

“VA issued the rule to clarify existing policy and protect Veterans’ benefits in the wake of an ongoing court action. But many interpreted the rule as something that could result in adverse consequences,” Collins said.

The federal rule, which went into effect Tuesday with no warning, drew swift outrage from major veterans groups who argued it could lower disability compensation for millions of veterans who suffer from physical ailments such as bone or muscle injuries and hypertension or mental health issues.

Chief among the concerns was that the change could lead to veterans choosing not to take medication to be rated higher and receive larger compensation.

“The VA’s response to the well-articulated concerns raised by so many has been dismissive and unacceptable,” said Veterans of Foreign Wars National Commander Carol Whitmore on Thursday. “Disabled veterans should never be forced to choose between following their doctor’s orders and protecting their earned benefits. This interim rule puts that stability at risk, and it must be withdrawn.”

Disabled American Veterans, meanwhile, said Tuesday it was “extremely disappointed and alarmed” by VA’s initial decision, which it said was developed and issued “in a closed and unnecessarily expedited process that effectively shut out veterans from providing any meaningful input.”

Six million veterans are currently receiving disability compensation, many of whom are on at least one medication, according to DAV.

“No veteran should ever have to worry that taking medications they need due to their illnesses and injuries could result in the reduction of their benefits,” according to DAV head Coleman Nee.

Recent court rulings had ordered the VA to base a veteran’s disability level on the severity of their condition, though the since-reversed VA rule required examiners to calculate any improvements veterans had from taking medication when determining ratings.

“While VA does not agree with the way this rule has been characterized, the department always takes Veterans’ concerns seriously,” Collins said in his statement. 

He added that to alleviate concerns, the VA will continue to collect public comments regarding the rule, a portal that opened Tuesday on regulations.gov that will run through April 20. 

Collins also said the VA would not enforce the rule “at any time in the future.” 

“Under my leadership, VA is ensuring that everyone applying for benefits receives everything they have earned as quickly and conveniently as possible,” he said. “We will continue these improvements on behalf of America’s Veterans, families, caregivers and survivors.”

After Collins’s announcement, DAV said it appreciates the secretary “listening to and acting on the concerns of those who have served and sacrificed for this nation.”

data.title: Johnson says Congress, admin will 'determine the best path forward' after SCOTUS tariff ruling
data.description:
Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) said Congress and the White House will “determine the best path forward in the coming weeks” after the Supreme Court struck down the bulk of President Trump’s sweeping tariffs on Friday in a rare rebuke.

“No one can deny that the President’s use of tariffs has brought in billions of dollars and created immense leverage for America’s trade strategy and for securing strong, reciprocal America-first trade agreements with countries that had been taking advantage of American workers for decades,” Johnson wrote on X. “Congress and the Administration will determine the best path forward in the coming weeks.”

The Supreme Court justices rejected Trump’s expanded use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) in imposing tariffs on nearly every country. The 1970s-era law allows the president to “regulate” imports when necessary to respond to national emergencies that pose an “unusual and extraordinary” threat. Trump is the first president to attempt to invoke the IEEPA to impose tariffs in its nearly 50-year history.  

“We claim no special competence in matters of economics or foreign affairs,” Chief Justice John Roberts wrote in the majority opinion. “We claim only, as we must, the limited role assigned to us by Article III of the Constitution. Fulfilling that role, we hold that IEEPA does not authorize the President to impose tariffs.” 

A few GOP tariff skeptics celebrated the Supreme Court’s ruling. Rep. Don Bacon (R-Neb.), for instance, called the Supreme Court’s ruling “common-sense” and “straightforward,” arguing that Article One of the Constitution gives tariff authority to Congress. 

“I feel vindicated as I’ve been saying this for the last 12 months. In the future, Congress should defend its own authorities and not rely on the Supreme Court. Besides the Constitutional concerns I had on the Administration’s broad-based tariffs, I also do not think tariffs are smart economic policy. Broad-based tariffs are bad economics,” Bacon wrote on X.

Six House Republicans, including Bacon, had joined Democrats to pass a resolution that would repeal Trump’s tariffs on Canada just last week. 

“Today’s decision by the Supreme Court reaffirms Congress’s authority under Article I of the Constitution to levy tariffs and restores balance between the legislative and executive branches,” Rep. Dan Newhouse (R-Wash.), who also voted for that resolution, wrote on X. 

“Tariffs can be effective in securing new trade deals when used in a targeted manner. I remain committed to working with President Trumps’ administration to secure trade deals that put American farmers, businesses, and consumers first,” he added. 

Trump held a snap press briefing on Friday afternoon to rail against the ruling.

Zach Schonfeld and Ella Lee contributed.

data.title: Will Democrats allow a school choice wealth transfer?
data.description:
It’s no secret that Democratic leaders despise school choice, usually at the behest of their teachers union backers. But do they hate it so much, they’ll let a huge amount of their home states’ wealth flow to Republican competitors?

That’s the question liberal governors face from California to Connecticut to almost every blue state in between. They’re deciding whether to opt into the federal school-choice program that Donald Trump and congressional Republicans created last year. The program opens in 2027, at which point any American can annually donate up to $1,700 to organizations that offer scholarships to students attending public or private schools. In return, the donor will get a dollar-for-credit tax credit, lowering or even eliminating their tax bill for the year.

Twenty-five states with Republican governors have said they’ll participate, which is what you’d expect from the party of school choice. But only one blue-state governor has said the same: Colorado’s Jared Polis. Ostensibly moderate Democrats, like Pennsylvania’s Josh Shapiro, haven’t said what they’ll do. Neither have more standard liberals like California Gov. Gavin Newsom. Democrats in New Mexico, Oregon and Wisconsin have already said no thanks — their states won’t let the scholarships come into their states. Democratic governors in Kansas and North Carolina are blocking attempts to enter the program by their Republican state legislatures, though veto overrides are possible.

But Polis is the smart one, or maybe just the least spiteful. His spokesman said in December that the “tax credit creates an immense opportunity for Coloradans to support students in our state.” Left unsaid was that if the governor didn’t opt in, Colorado taxpayers could still claim the tax credit by donating to scholarship-granting organizations. But they could have only donated to groups in states that participate in the program — i.e., not Colorado.

The result would have been a potentially enormous wealth transfer out of the Rocky Mountain State — not only to neighbors like Wyoming, but also to Texas, Florida and other deep-red states. According to conservative projections published by Education Reform Now, if just 1 percent of Colorado filers with more than $1,700 in federal tax liabilities claimed the credit, the state would lose $17 million a year. If 30 percent donated, more than $500 million would leave the state annually.

By opting into the tax-credit program, Polis is keeping Colorado’s money in his own state. Some residents may still give to groups in other states, but realistically, they’ll donate to organizations close to home, where they have the strongest connections and can see the most impact. It’s human nature. But it’s also human nature to lower your tax bill however you can. If Coloradans can’t do it while supporting students down the street, they’ll do it for the benefit of students as far away as south Florida.

Jared Polis shouldn’t be mistaken for a school-choice fan, but he does recognize that Colorado ought to come first. Other Democratic governors are putting their states last, either intentionally or through indecision. If they don’t opt into the tax-credit program, they’ll lose enormous sums to competitors.

Take California, where Gavin Newsom has been silent on the scholarships. Education Reform Now conservatively estimates the state could lose up to $3.1 billion a year in scholarship donations to places like Texas, Tennessee and North Carolina. In Pennsylvania, Josh Shapiro could see as much as $967 million leave the state annually, mostly heading south and west. New York, New Jersey, Connecticut, and Michigan are already losing residents to sunnier states. Do they also want those who remain to send billions of dollars to the Sunshine State, too? They may even lose more residents, as families move to places where they can apply for scholarships under the program.

When the tax-credit program goes into effect next year, it’s unlikely that everyone who can donate will. But even if blue states have a take-up rate of between 1 percent and 5 percent, they’ll still see hundreds of millions of dollars benefit students who live outside their borders. The only way to keep that money in their states is for Democratic governors to opt into the program. No doubt, their teachers union backers will scream and shout and threaten them. But surely that’s better than watching more of their people’s wealth move to competing states that are already pulling ahead.

Christian Barnard is a senior fellow at the Foundation for Government Accountability.

data.title: Chamber of Commerce: Tariffs ruling 'welcome news' for businesses, consumers
data.description:
The U.S. Chamber of Commerce praised the Supreme Court’s Friday ruling, which halted the bulk of President Trump’s tariffs, as “welcome news” for businesses and consumers. 

“Over the past year, the Chamber has been working with small and midsize businesses around the country that have seen significant cost increases and supply chain disruptions as a result of these tariffs,” Executive Vice President and Chief Policy Officer Neil Bradley said in a statement on behalf of the Chamber. 

“Swift refunds of the impermissible tariffs will be meaningful for the more than 200,000 small business importers in this country and will help support stronger economic growth this year,” he said.

On Friday, Supreme Court justices rejected as unlawful Trump’s expanded use of the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to impose tariffs on most countries. 

“We claim only, as we must, the limited role assigned to us by Article III of the Constitution,” Chief Justice John Roberts wrote in the decision. “Fulfilling that role, we hold that IEEPA does not authorize the President to impose tariffs.”

The Chamber, which represents the interests of nearly 3 million companies, has been critical of Trump’s tariffs from the get-go. In July, it filed an amicus brief arguing the president was overstepping his statutory authority and his actions were causing “irreparable harm to businesses of all sizes.”

“We encourage the administration to use this opportunity to reset overall tariff policy in a manner that will lead to greater economic growth, larger wage gains for workers, and lower costs for families,” Bradley said in the statement.

data.title: Pence praises tariff ruling as win for Americans and separation of powers
data.description:
Former Vice President Mike Pence (R) lauded the Supreme Court’s decision on Friday to strike down many of the Trump administration’s tariffs.

A 6-3 majority of the court’s justices ruled that President Trump unlawfully used an emergency statute to impose far-reaching tariffs on American trading partners. 

“In Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump, our Supreme Court has reaffirmed that the Constitution grants Congress – not the President – the power to tax,” Pence posted on the social platform X. “American families and American businesses pay American tariffs – not foreign countries. With this decision, American families and businesses can breathe a sigh of relief.”

Following his departure from the White House, Pence has become a regular critic of the president and his economic policies. 

Pence had previously warned of the risks these proposed policies could have on U.S. consumers and the economy. In an exclusive interview with The Hill after Trump’s tariff announcement last April, the former vice president called the move a “misstep.”

“I don’t think the American people were voting for what would amount to the largest peacetime tax hike in American history, which, the tariffs that were announced last week, if left in place, would certainly be, and the hardship they’d place on working families and businesses large and small,” he said at the time.

Pence is the founder of the conservative organization Advancing American Freedom. The organization’s amicus brief program collaborated on the case against Trump’s tariffs, Pence said. 

“With this historic decision, America can now return to the pursuit of Free Trade with Free Nations under the Constitution of the United States!” Pence wrote on X.

data.title: Live updates: Trump pans Supreme Court tariffs ruling as 'deeply disappointing'
data.description:
The Supreme Court dealt a crushing blow to President Trump’s tariff agenda, ruling on Friday that his tariff scheme is illegal without congressional authorization. 

In one of the biggest cases justices have decided in years, the majority conservative court issued a rare rebuke of the president’s actions. Chief Justice John Roberts and Justices Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett — the latter two appointed by Trump — joining the three liberal justices in striking down the levies that were based on an international economic emergency.

In a press briefing, Trump called the ruling “deeply disappointing” and said he “was ashamed.” He congratulated the conservative justices who backed him.

Meanwhile, Trump hosted governors at the White House for breakfast Friday, but not under the umbrella of the National Governors Association (NGA), whose meeting is underway in Washington. Trump has tangled with two Democratic governors — Wes Moore of Maryland and Jared Polis of Colorado — which cast a shadow on the breakfast and Saturday night’s annual dinner. Both decided to attend the meeting, reversing course.

Follow live below.

data.title: Big Tech should sell America on AI data centers, not the other way around
data.description:
AI data centers are essential to winning the global AI race — but most communities don’t want them in their backyards. It’s easy to see why. These facilities are enormous, resource-intensive and often difficult for communities to digest.

Yet these anxieties, while real, cannot become a pretext for retreat. If the U.S. wants to win the AI race — economically, militarily and for national prosperity — we must build public support for data centers.

The broader public feels this tension keenly. Nationwide surveys show a striking paradox: While 93 percent of Americans agree that AI data centers are vital to U.S. competitiveness, a strong majority would rather not have them near their community. They are concerned about energy, water and land use even as they recognize the strategic stakes. Only about 9 percent of respondents believe the local economic benefits clearly outweigh the perceived drawbacks, while large shares express concern about water and energy consumption.

This disconnect — strong national support coupled with local ambivalence or opposition — tells us something important: Data centers may be necessary in the global AI race, but they are not popular. Achieving public acceptance requires intentional design and community investment on four fronts: job creation, tax treatment, energy and water.

Critics point out that a fully built-out data center may require only a handful of technicians to maintain servers. Although this is true, we must remember that AI data centers are not static; they are expanding, iterating and upgrading continually as AI grows. Unlike a one-off factory build, the lifecycle of a data center often unfolds in multiple phases over years, providing sustained construction jobs and contracting opportunities. 

Each new generation of AI hardware demands new facilities, and each facility is complex, requiring electricians, engineers, technicians and logistics support. The result is long-term, recurring employment, even if the operational headcount remains modest. As recent economic analyses have shown, a large data center supports thousands of construction jobs and hundreds of millions in local economic activity over extended buildouts.

When it comes to taxes, AI companies should be wary of accepting lavish tax incentives. Accepting large rebates may pad the bottom line in the short term, but it sends the wrong signal — it positions data centers as extractive, rather than contributive. Instead, AI firms should refuse tax kickbacks and instead commit to be a net positive for local governments.

This approach has two advantages. First, it strengthens public trust: residents see the company as an investment, not a burden. Second, it aligns with economic reality: If AI is as transformative as the projections suggest it will be, these companies will be highly profitable. Rather than chasing temporary tax relief, they can enhance the local tax base, funding schools, parks, athletics, the arts and other community programs. Communities gain not just jobs but enduring fiscal benefits.

On energy, critics are correct in noting the strain on local grids and the potential rise in electricity prices. Yet this challenge is also an opportunity to innovate in energy production and distribution.

As I recently argued, the best path forward is for data centers to generate their own electricity and, where possible, contribute surplus to the grid. Doing so would not only curb price increases for consumers, but potentially even reduce electricity costs. By integrating zero-emissions small modular reactors, solar arrays, wind, geothermal, battery storage and other energy innovations into facilities, data centers can become a net positive for communities. Done right, this could stabilize local energy prices and increase grid resilience for residents.

Finally, data centers can demand millions of gallons of water annually, straining local supplies. But even here, there is a path to positive impact.

At a recent Microsoft event I attended, the company showcased a facility designed to replenish more water than it consumes. The strategy involved implementing water efficiency upgrades and recycling systems, and paying to repair local infrastructure to prevent current leaks and waste loss. Microsoft’s stated intention was to make the data center a net contributor to local water resources, fixing a local problem that would have persisted without the data center’s presence.

I hope Microsoft succeeds with this project, and that other AI companies adopt similar practices. Proactive water stewardship not only mitigates environmental concerns but also demonstrates that these facilities should not just be tolerated, but valued.

Ultimately, America must win the AI race, and to do that, we must build data centers. Communities are right to demand that data centers do more good than harm for local residents, and technology companies should welcome spreading their gains to the people they rely on to build the AI age.

They can do this by having the right approach to jobs, taxes, energy and water. If done right, data centers can become engines of growth, stability and innovation that not only help America lead in the AI race, but also win the trust of the American people along the way.

Drew Bond is the co-founder and executive chairman of C3 Solutions.

data.title: Moreno calls for GOP to codify scuttled Trump tariffs
data.description:
Sen. Bernie Moreno (R-Ohio) called for Republicans in Congress to codify President Trump’s tariffs after the Supreme Court on Friday invalidated the levies.

“SCOTUS’s outrageous ruling handcuffs our fight against unfair trade that has devastated American workers for decades,” Moreno said in a statement. “These tariffs protected jobs, revived manufacturing, and forced cheaters like China to pay up. Now globalists win, factories investments may reverse, and American workers lose again. 

“This betrayal must be reversed and Republicans must get to work immediately on a reconciliation bill to codify the tariffs that had made our country the hottest country on earth!” he added.

The Ohio Republican, who is a member of Senate Majority Whip John Barrasso’s (R-Wyo.) whip team, made the call as Republicans question the possibility of moving forward on a second reconciliation package less than a year after they used the process to pass the “big, beautiful bill” last summer. Budget reconciliation allows the majority party to bypass the Senate filibuster to pass a bill, but it is an arduous process with a number of constraints.

Some Senate Republicans have called for action ahead of the midterms to help drive down costs for Americans or to deal with health care, defense or housing policy, among other items. 

That debate has sparked a divide as others believe that Republicans’ focus in coming months must be on selling the massive tax cut package they already passed, which the party has acknowledged it must do more to promote.

Any push to codify Trump’s tariffs through reconciliation would likely be a contentious one given that the levies are a divisive issue in the Senate GOP. Republican opponents of the tariffs argue they represent a harmful tax on Americans and that the power to tax lies with Congress.

Four Republicans joined Democrats last year in voting to repeal a wide swath of Trump’s global tariffs, and several GOP senators, including Rand Paul (Ky.) and Mitch McConnell (Ky.) celebrated the Supreme Court decision on Friday.

The House GOP’s one-vote margin also means getting any bill of consequence over the line would be extremely difficult. 

The court’s 6-3 ruling marked a major blow to Trump’s economic efforts, with the president warning in recent months that striking them down would be a financial calamity for the U.S. given the tens of billions collected via these tariffs.

The tariffs in question were levied via the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), which had allowed him to slap them on individual countries rapidly. 

Chief Justice John Roberts wrote for the majority in the case, saying the IEEPA “does not authorize the President to impose tariffs.” 

“It is also telling that in IEEPA’s half century of existence, no President has invoked the statute to impose any tariffs, let alone tariffs of this magnitude and scope,” Roberts wrote. “Accordingly, the President must ‘point to clear congressional authorization’ to justify his extraordinary assertion of that power. He cannot.”
